description: A screenshot of a Qwen model card on Hugging Face Hub, showing evaluation results and metadata including benchmark scores, model capabilities, and performance metrics as reported by the model author.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the structure and content of a model card, helping students understand how evaluation results are presented by model authors, what metrics are typically reported, and how to interpret author-reported benchmarks versus community leaderboards.
when_to_show: Show when teaching students the difference between author-reported evaluation scores on model cards versus community-driven leaderboard scores; use to illustrate how to read and critically assess model capability claims.
suggested_caption: Qwen model card showing author-reported evaluation results on Hugging Face Hub.

description: A taxonomy diagram showing the threat landscape for LLM-based applications, including prompt injection, jailbreaks, data extraction, and indirect attacks via external content sources like web pages and plugins.
teaching_value: Gives students a structured mental map of the attack surface before diving into specific techniques, helping them categorize and contextualize each attack type they will encounter.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on LLM security to orient students before introducing specific attack methods.
suggested_caption: Taxonomy of adversarial threats to LLM applications. (Greshake et al. 2023)

description: A probability density plot of the Gumbel(0,1) distribution, showing its characteristic right-skewed shape with a sharp peak near 0 and a long right tail.
teaching_value: Helps students visualize the asymmetric shape of the Gumbel distribution before understanding how Gumbel noise is used to approximate categorical sampling in gradient-based text attacks.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the Gumbel-softmax reparameterization trick, immediately before explaining how it enables differentiable approximation of discrete token sampling.
suggested_caption: Probability density of Gumbel(0,1) used in softmax reparameterization. (ChatGPT)

description: A multi-panel figure from Jang et al. 2016 showing how Gumbel-softmax distributions behave at different temperatures (τ→0 approaches one-hot categorical; τ→∞ approaches uniform), plus plots comparing expectations and samples from the distribution.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates the temperature parameter's role in controlling the trade-off between discrete (categorical) and continuous (uniform) distributions, which is critical for understanding how gradient signals flow through the approximation during adversarial optimization.
when_to_show: Show after introducing the Gumbel(0,1) density plot, when explaining how temperature τ controls the smoothness of the approximation and why τ→0 recovers the original discrete distribution.
suggested_caption: Gumbel-softmax at varying temperatures: τ→0 is categorical, τ→∞ is uniform. (Jang et al. 2016)

description: A diagram illustrating the Universal Adversarial Triggers (UAT) method: a short trigger phrase is prepended to any input, causing the model to produce a targeted incorrect output regardless of the actual input content.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the input-agnostic nature of UAT — the same trigger works across diverse inputs — helping students understand why this attack is powerful and transferable.
when_to_show: Show when first introducing UAT to illustrate the concept of a universal trigger before explaining the gradient-based search algorithm used to find it.
suggested_caption: Universal Adversarial Triggers prepended to any input force targeted model outputs. (Wallace et al. 2019)

description: A table of example UAT trigger phrases for different NLP tasks (sentiment classification, reading comprehension, conditional text generation), showing the actual learned trigger tokens and the resulting model outputs.
teaching_value: Provides concrete, empirical evidence of what UAT triggers look like in practice — often nonsensical token sequences — helping students understand both the effectiveness and the detectability weakness of gradient-based triggers.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the UAT algorithm to ground the theory in real examples, and use the nonsensical trigger tokens to motivate the discussion of perplexity-based detection.
suggested_caption: Example UAT triggers for sentiment, QA, and text generation tasks. (Wallace et al. 2019)

description: A flowchart illustrating the UTSC (Unigram Trigger with Selection Criteria) pipeline: unigram UAT tokens are generated, passed with conversation history to an LLM to produce attack utterances, then filtered by toxicity classifiers.
teaching_value: Shows how combining gradient-based token search with LLM generation and toxicity filtering produces more fluent, lower-perplexity attacks, illustrating the trade-off between attack fluency and detectability.
when_to_show: Show when comparing UAT variants, specifically to contrast UTSC's multi-step pipeline with the simpler UAT approach and explain why lower perplexity matters for evading defenses.
suggested_caption: UTSC pipeline: unigram triggers → LLM generation → toxicity-filtered attack utterances. (Mehrabi et al. 2022)

description: A bar chart comparing attack success rates of UAT, UAT-LM, UTSC-1, UTSC-2, and UTSC-3 as measured by multiple toxicity classifiers (Perspective API, Safety classifier, etc.) against a defender model.
teaching_value: Enables quantitative comparison of attack variants, showing students how different design choices (language model loss constraint, selection criteria) affect real-world attack effectiveness across different evaluation metrics.
when_to_show: Show after explaining all UAT variants to summarize their relative performance and prompt discussion about which metrics best capture attack quality.
suggested_caption: Attack success rates of UAT variants measured by multiple toxicity classifiers. (Mehrabi et al. 2022)

description: A diagram showing where adversarial suffix tokens (marked with red exclamation points) are appended to a user instruction in the prompt, illustrating the GCG (Greedy Coordinate Gradient) attack setup from Zou et al. 2023.
teaching_value: Clearly shows the structural placement of adversarial tokens as a suffix rather than a prefix, helping students understand the GCG attack's formulation and how it differs from UAT's prepended trigger approach.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the Zou et al. GCG method to visually anchor where the learned adversarial tokens appear in the prompt before explaining the optimization procedure.
suggested_caption: Red tokens mark adversarial suffix positions learned by GCG optimization. (Zou et al. 2023)

description: A results table showing average attack success rates of GCG adversarial suffixes on Vicuna-7b and Vicuna-13b for harmful behavior instructions, comparing baselines, single suffixes, concatenated suffixes, and ensemble strategies.
teaching_value: Demonstrates empirically how combining multiple adversarial suffixes (concatenation and ensemble) dramatically increases attack success rates, teaching students about the compounding effect of adversarial strategies.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the GCG optimization algorithm to present its empirical effectiveness and motivate discussion of why concatenation and ensemble strategies boost success rates.
suggested_caption: GCG suffix attack success rates on Vicuna models; concatenation boosts performance. (Zou et al. 2023)

description: A bar chart comparing average success rates of ARCA and baseline methods (UAT, AutoPrompt) at triggering GPT-2 and GPT-J to produce toxic outputs, broken down by output length (1, 2, 3 tokens) and full CivilComments dataset.
teaching_value: Provides quantitative evidence of ARCA's advantage over prior gradient-based methods, helping students understand how the decomposition of the score function into linear and autoregressive terms improves attack effectiveness.
when_to_show: Show after explaining ARCA's score decomposition technique to validate why the approximation improvement matters in practice compared to UAT and AutoPrompt baselines.
suggested_caption: ARCA outperforms UAT and AutoPrompt at triggering toxic outputs in GPT-2/GPT-J. (Jones et al. 2023)

description: A table or chart showing various jailbreak prompt types (e.g., role-playing, prompt injection, obfuscation) and their empirical success rates against LLMs, organized by attack category and model tested.
teaching_value: Helps students understand the taxonomy of jailbreak strategies and quantitatively compare their effectiveness, illustrating that safety alignment can be bypassed through specific prompt engineering techniques.
when_to_show: Show when introducing jailbreak attacks to contrast different attack strategies and motivate why robust alignment is difficult; useful for discussing the two failure modes of LLM safety from Wei et al. 2023.
suggested_caption: Jailbreak attack types and success rates across LLMs. (Wei et al. 2023)

description: A screenshot of an adversarial writing interface showing top-5 model predictions on the upper left and a user-facing question editor on the lower right with words color-highlighted by their importance score to the model's prediction.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how human-in-the-loop adversarial tools work by surfacing word-level saliency to guide humans in crafting inputs that fool NLP models, making the concept of human-assisted red-teaming tangible.
when_to_show: Show when explaining human-assisted adversarial attack tooling and how interpretability features (word importance) can be weaponized to help humans find model failure cases more efficiently.
suggested_caption: Adversarial writing UI with word importance highlights guiding human attackers. (Wallace et al. 2019)

description: A UI screenshot showing a tool where human annotators edit prompts or model completions to reduce a safety classifier's predicted probability of violent content, with probability scores displayed in real time.
teaching_value: Demonstrates how tool-assisted human red-teaming works in practice for safety classifiers, showing the feedback loop between human edits and classifier scores that accelerates finding adversarial examples.
when_to_show: Show when discussing Ziegler et al. 2022's approach to finding classifier failures; contrast with fully automated red-teaming to highlight the complementary role of human judgment.
suggested_caption: Human red-teaming UI for lowering safety classifier scores on violent content. (Ziegler et al. 2022)

description: A scatter plot with attack success rate (% offensive responses) on the x-axis and sample diversity (self-BLEU) on the y-axis, with nodes representing different red-team generation methods (ZS, SFS, SL, BAD, RL variants) colored from blue (low offensiveness) to red (high offensiveness).
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the fundamental tradeoff between attack success rate and diversity in red-teaming, showing that RL-based methods achieve high attack success but collapse diversity, while zero-shot preserves diversity at the cost of effectiveness.
when_to_show: Show when teaching the tradeoffs in automated red-teaming strategies; use to motivate why no single method dominates and why diversity metrics matter alongside success rate.
suggested_caption: Tradeoff between attack success rate and diversity across red-team methods. (Perez et al. 2022)

description: A pipeline diagram illustrating three sequential phases of human-in-the-loop red-teaming: Explore (discovering model behaviors), Establish (confirming failure modes), and Exploit (generating adversarial examples at scale).
teaching_value: Provides a structured mental model for how systematic red-teaming is organized, helping students understand that effective adversarial testing requires iterative exploration before exploitation.
when_to_show: Show when introducing structured red-teaming workflows; use as a framework students can apply when thinking about how to systematically probe LLM safety.
suggested_caption: Explore-Establish-Exploit pipeline for structured human-in-the-loop red-teaming. (Casper et al. 2023)

description: A table showing attack effectiveness (percentage of prompts triggering unsafe image generations) for different FLIRT attack strategies across multiple diffusion models, with SFS as a baseline and unique prompt percentages in parentheses.
teaching_value: Quantitatively shows how in-context learning-based red-teaming (FLIRT) compares to baseline methods for attacking image generation models, illustrating that feedback-loop prompting significantly increases unsafe generation rates.
when_to_show: Show when extending adversarial attack concepts from text LLMs to multimodal/image generation models, and when discussing how in-context learning can be used offensively.
suggested_caption: FLIRT attack effectiveness on diffusion models vs. baseline strategies. (Mehrabi et al. 2023)

description: A visualization related to the saddle point / robust optimization framework for adversarial robustness, likely showing decision boundaries or loss landscapes illustrating how model capacity affects robustness to adversarial perturbations.
teaching_value: Illustrates the mathematical intuition behind robust optimization as a min-max (saddle point) problem, and shows empirically that larger model capacity improves adversarial robustness even without data augmentation.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the formal mathematical framing of adversarial robustness; use to connect the intuitive notion of adversarial attacks to the bi-level optimization objective.
suggested_caption: Saddle point robust optimization: larger capacity improves adversarial decision boundaries. (Madry et al. 2017)

description: A bar chart showing 'PPL Passed' and 'PPL Window Passed' rates — the fraction of adversarial prompts (with suffixes from Zou et al. 2023) that bypass a perplexity-based filter — across different experimental conditions.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the effectiveness of perplexity filtering as a defense against gradient-based adversarial suffix attacks, helping students understand both the defense mechanism and its limitations quantitatively.
when_to_show: Show when discussing defenses against adversarial attacks on LLMs, specifically to illustrate how perplexity-based filtering can detect unnatural adversarial suffixes and when it fails.
suggested_caption: Perplexity filter bypass rates for adversarial suffix attacks. (Jain et al. 2023)

description: A two-axis diagram organizing seven LLM system design patterns along two spectrums: 'closer to data' vs 'closer to user' (horizontal) and 'reducing cost/risk' vs 'improving performance' (vertical), with labeled connections between patterns.
teaching_value: Gives students a mental map of how LLM engineering patterns relate to each other and where to apply them, making abstract architectural choices concrete and navigable.
when_to_show: Show at the start of any lesson on LLM system design to orient students before diving into individual patterns.
suggested_caption: Seven LLM patterns mapped by data proximity and performance/cost tradeoff.

description: Side-by-side visual comparison of BERTScore (left, showing one-to-one 'hard' token alignment between reference and generated text) and MoverScore (right, showing many-to-one 'soft' token alignment using optimal transport).
teaching_value: Visually contrasts hard vs. soft alignment strategies in text evaluation metrics, helping students understand why MoverScore can better capture semantic similarity than BERTScore.
when_to_show: Show when teaching the limitations of exact-match metrics and introducing embedding-based evaluation; use to explain the tradeoff between precision and semantic flexibility.
suggested_caption: BERTScore uses hard alignment (left); MoverScore uses soft alignment (right).

alt: Dimensions of model evaluations with ROUGE
description: A multi-dimensional radar or bar chart showing how ROUGE scores vary across different evaluation dimensions, illustrating the metric's inconsistency and limited adaptability across tasks.
teaching_value: Concretely demonstrates the multi-dimensional weaknesses of ROUGE, helping students understand why a single metric can be misleading and why task-specific evaluation matters.
when_to_show: Show when discussing the pitfalls of standard NLP metrics, especially before introducing more robust or task-specific evaluation approaches.
suggested_caption: ROUGE scores vary widely across evaluation dimensions, revealing metric limitations.

alt: Different prompts for the same question across MMLU implementations
description: A table or side-by-side comparison showing the same MMLU question formatted with different prompts across three implementations: original MMLU, HELM, and EleutherAI, highlighting prompt wording differences.
teaching_value: Demonstrates concretely how prompt variation alone can cause the same model to receive different benchmark scores, teaching students to be skeptical of benchmark comparisons.
when_to_show: Show when discussing benchmark reliability or when students assume leaderboard scores are directly comparable across implementations.
suggested_caption: Same MMLU question, three different prompts across benchmark implementations.

alt: Different eval for the same question across MMLU implementations
description: A comparison table or diagram showing how the evaluation methodology (e.g., answer extraction, scoring approach) differs for the same MMLU question across original, HELM, and EleutherAI implementations.
teaching_value: Illustrates that beyond prompt differences, evaluation logic itself varies across implementations, reinforcing why benchmark scores are not directly comparable without identical setups.
when_to_show: Show immediately after Image 5 to compound the lesson: both prompts AND evaluation logic differ, making cross-benchmark comparisons unreliable.
suggested_caption: Evaluation logic differs across MMLU implementations, not just prompts.

description: A flowchart overview of the G-Eval framework showing: (1) task introduction + criteria fed to LLM to generate Chain-of-Thought evaluation steps, (2) concatenation of prompt, CoT, article, and summary, (3) LLM outputs a score, and (4) token probabilities used for weighted score normalization.
teaching_value: Shows students a concrete architecture for LLM-as-judge evaluation, illustrating how CoT reasoning and probability weighting can replace human annotators or gold references.
when_to_show: Show when introducing LLM-based evaluation frameworks, especially to contrast with traditional metrics like BLEU/ROUGE and human evaluation.
suggested_caption: G-Eval uses CoT and token probabilities for reference-free LLM evaluation.

description: An architecture diagram of Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) showing: a document index built with DPR encoders, a retriever fetching relevant documents at inference time, concatenation of input with retrieved documents, and a BART LLM generating output tokens.
teaching_value: Provides a clear architectural reference for RAG, helping students understand how parametric (LLM) and non-parametric (retrieval) components interact to reduce hallucination and enable knowledge updates.
when_to_show: Show at the introduction of the RAG pattern to ground students in the original architecture before discussing variants or optimizations.
suggested_caption: RAG combines dense retrieval with a generative LLM to reduce hallucinations.

description: An architecture diagram of Fusion-in-Decoder (FiD) showing how multiple retrieved documents are each independently encoded (fused in the encoder) and then jointly decoded by a single generative decoder to produce the answer.
teaching_value: Contrasts FiD's encode-separately/decode-jointly approach with RAG's concatenation approach, helping students understand how multi-document fusion improves open-domain QA.
when_to_show: Show after introducing RAG to illustrate an architectural variant that handles multiple retrieved documents more efficiently.
suggested_caption: FiD encodes each retrieved document separately, then decodes jointly.

description: An architecture diagram of RETRO (Retrieval-Enhanced Transformer) showing chunked input processing, frozen BERT retriever fetching neighbors per chunk, a differentiable encoder, and chunked cross-attention integrated throughout pre-training and inference.
teaching_value: Illustrates how retrieval can be integrated at pre-training time and at a finer chunk granularity, helping students understand RETRO's architectural innovations over inference-only RAG.
when_to_show: Show when comparing RAG variants, especially to highlight the distinction between inference-time retrieval (RAG) and pre-training-integrated retrieval (RETRO).
suggested_caption: RETRO integrates chunked retrieval throughout pre-training via cross-attention.

alt: Performance from RETRO-fitting a pre-trained model
description: Bar chart comparing perplexity scores of baseline models, RETRO-fitted models, and RETRO trained from scratch, showing that RETRO-fitting (freezing pre-trained weights and training only chunked cross-attention and neighbor encoder) achieves performance close to full RETRO training.
teaching_value: Helps students understand the efficiency trade-off in RETRO-fitting: by training less than 10% of parameters, models can approach the performance of fully trained retrieval-augmented models, illustrating parameter-efficient fine-tuning in the RAG context.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how existing pre-trained models can be upgraded with retrieval capabilities without full retraining, especially when discussing cost-performance trade-offs in RAG systems.
suggested_caption: RETRO-fitting achieves near-scratch RETRO performance by training <10% of weights. (Source: RETRO paper)

description: Architecture diagram showing the pipeline of internet-augmented LLMs: a query triggers a Google Search, retrieved documents are chunked into paragraphs, ranked via TF-IDF and cosine similarity, and top paragraphs are used as few-shot evidence to condition the LLM.
teaching_value: Illustrates a concrete, practical RAG pipeline using off-the-shelf tools (Google Search, TF-IDF), helping students understand how retrieval, chunking, ranking, and prompting integrate into a full system.
when_to_show: Show when introducing RAG pipelines to students, particularly to contrast lightweight retrieval methods (TF-IDF) with dense embedding approaches, or when discussing few-shot prompting with retrieved evidence.
suggested_caption: Internet-augmented LLMs retrieve, chunk, rank, then prompt with evidence. (Source: Internet-augmented LMs paper)

description: Diagram showing three evaluation settings for CodeT5+ with RAG: generative-only, retrieval-only, and retrieval-augmented generation, illustrating how retrieved code snippets are combined with the generative model for code generation tasks.
teaching_value: Demonstrates RAG applied beyond QA to code generation, helping students see how retrieval provides domain-specific context (e.g., library usage) that pure generation misses, reinforcing the general applicability of RAG.
when_to_show: Show when discussing RAG applications beyond question answering, or when illustrating how retrieved context improves correctness in structured generation tasks like code.
suggested_caption: RAG for CodeT5+: retrieval provides crucial library context for code generation. (Source: CodeT5+ paper)

description: Flowchart of Hypothetical Document Embeddings (HyDE): a query is fed to an LLM (e.g., InstructGPT) to generate a hypothetical document, which is then encoded by an unsupervised encoder (e.g., Contriever) to retrieve real documents via embedding similarity.
teaching_value: Teaches students how to bridge the query-document embedding gap without labeled relevance data, illustrating a creative use of LLM generation to improve zero-shot retrieval.
when_to_show: Show when teaching dense retrieval limitations (query-document mismatch) and creative solutions, or when discussing zero-shot and unsupervised retrieval methods in RAG pipelines.
suggested_caption: HyDE generates a hypothetical document to bridge query-document embedding gap. (Source: HyDE paper)

description: Three-stage diagram of ULMFiT: (1) LM pre-training on Wikitext-103, (2) LM fine-tuning on target domain data, and (3) classifier fine-tuning with added linear layers on the target task, using AWD-LSTM architecture.
teaching_value: Establishes the foundational pre-train → fine-tune paradigm for NLP transfer learning, helping students understand how the protocol originated before Transformers and why each stage matters.
when_to_show: Show at the start of a fine-tuning module to establish historical context and the three-stage transfer learning protocol before introducing BERT and GPT.
suggested_caption: ULMFiT: pre-train, domain fine-tune, then classifier fine-tune — foundational NLP transfer learning. (Source: ULMFiT paper)

description: Diagram showing BERT's pre-training objectives (masked language modeling and next sentence prediction) and its fine-tuning on downstream tasks such as NER, Q&A, and classification, highlighting the encoder-only architecture.
teaching_value: Illustrates how BERT's bidirectional pre-training objectives differ from unidirectional models, and how a single pre-trained model can be fine-tuned for diverse NLP tasks by adding task-specific heads.
when_to_show: Show when explaining BERT's architecture and training objectives, especially to contrast with GPT's unidirectional approach or to illustrate task-specific fine-tuning with classification heads.
suggested_caption: BERT pre-trains bidirectionally via MLM and NSP, then fine-tunes per task. (Source: BERT paper)

description: Diagram of GPT's pre-training on BooksCorpus via next-token prediction and fine-tuning pipeline for multiple tasks (classification, entailment, similarity, Q&A) using a decoder-only Transformer architecture with task-specific input transformations.
teaching_value: Shows how GPT uses a unified decoder-only architecture with task-specific input formatting rather than separate model heads, helping students understand the design philosophy that led to instruction-following models.
when_to_show: Show when contrasting GPT's unidirectional, decoder-only design with BERT's bidirectional encoder, or when tracing the lineage from GPT-1 to modern instruction-tuned models.
suggested_caption: GPT pre-trains via next-token prediction, then fine-tunes with task-specific input formats. (Source: GPT paper)

description: Diagram of T5's encoder-decoder architecture showing how all NLP tasks are reformulated as text-to-text problems using task-specific prefixes (e.g., 'Translate English to German:', 'Summarize:'), with pre-training on C4 using a denoising/masked language modeling objective.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the text-to-text unification paradigm, helping students understand how a single model and fine-tuning approach can handle diverse tasks, and introduces the concept of prompt prefixes as hyperparameters.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how T5 unifies NLP tasks under a single text-to-text framework, or when introducing prompt engineering as a concept that evolved from T5's prefix design.
suggested_caption: T5 unifies all NLP tasks as text-to-text using task-specific prefixes. (Source: T5 paper)

alt: Overview of fine-tuning steps in InstructGPT
description: Three-step diagram of InstructGPT's training pipeline: (1) supervised fine-tuning on human demonstrations, (2) reward model training from human preference comparisons, and (3) PPO-based reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to align the model with instructions.
teaching_value: Clearly illustrates the RLHF pipeline — SFT → reward modeling → PPO — helping students understand how human preferences are incorporated into LLM training to improve instruction-following and alignment.
when_to_show: Show when teaching RLHF and alignment techniques, or when explaining how InstructGPT/ChatGPT differ from base GPT-3 in terms of training methodology.
suggested_caption: InstructGPT: SFT → reward model → PPO RLHF pipeline for instruction alignment. (Source: InstructGPT paper)

description: Comparison diagram of prefix-tuning versus full fine-tuning: prefix-tuning prepends trainable prefix vectors to hidden states of all Transformer layers while keeping original LM weights frozen, contrasted with updating all parameters in full fine-tuning.
teaching_value: Visually distinguishes prefix-tuning from full fine-tuning and soft prompt tuning, helping students understand parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods and why updating only 0.1% of parameters can match full fine-tuning performance.
when_to_show: Show when comparing PEFT methods (prefix-tuning, prompt tuning, LoRA), especially to illustrate where in the model trainable parameters are inserted and the efficiency gains achieved.
suggested_caption: Prefix-tuning prepends trainable vectors to all layers, matching full fine-tuning at 0.1% parameters. (Source: Prefix-Tuning paper)

description: Diagram showing the adapter technique architecture: two fully connected network layers inserted into each transformer block — one after the attention layer and one after the feed-forward network layer, with the rest of the transformer weights frozen.
teaching_value: Visually clarifies where adapter modules are injected into a transformer block, helping students understand why only 3.6% of parameters need updating while achieving near full fine-tuning performance.
when_to_show: Show when introducing parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods, specifically to contrast adapter insertion points with full fine-tuning before moving to LoRA.
suggested_caption: Adapter layers inserted after attention and FFN in each transformer block. (Source: Houlsby et al.)

description: Diagram illustrating LoRA's mechanism: the original pre-trained weight matrix W is frozen, and a parallel low-rank decomposition (two matrices A and B of reduced rank r) is trained and added to the forward pass output.
teaching_value: Makes the low-rank decomposition concept concrete — students can see that only the small A and B matrices are trained, explaining the dramatic parameter reduction and implicit regularization effect.
when_to_show: Show after explaining adapter layers, when introducing LoRA as an alternative PEFT method; use to explain why low-rank updates provide implicit regularization.
suggested_caption: LoRA decomposes weight updates into two low-rank matrices A and B. (Source: Hu et al.)

description: Diagram comparing QLoRA to standard LoRA fine-tuning, illustrating the 4-bit quantized frozen base model, the 16-bit LoRA adapters trained on top, double quantization, and paged optimizers for memory management.
teaching_value: Shows how QLoRA stacks multiple memory-saving innovations (4-bit NormalFloat, double quantization, paged optimizers) on top of LoRA, helping students understand how a 65B model can be fine-tuned on consumer hardware.
when_to_show: Show after LoRA is understood, when discussing how to fine-tune very large models under GPU memory constraints; useful for comparing memory footprints.
suggested_caption: QLoRA combines 4-bit quantization with LoRA adapters to reduce fine-tuning memory. (Source: Dettmers et al.)

description: Architecture diagram of GPTCache showing the pipeline: incoming LLM requests are checked against a semantic cache using embedding similarity; cache hits return stored responses, cache misses are forwarded to the LLM and the result is stored.
teaching_value: Illustrates how semantic caching differs from exact-match caching — students can trace the request flow and understand why embedding-based similarity enables reuse of semantically equivalent (not just identical) queries.
when_to_show: Show when teaching LLM serving optimization or cost reduction strategies; use to contrast semantic caching with traditional key-value caching.
suggested_caption: GPTCache uses embedding similarity to serve cached LLM responses. (Source: GPTCache)

description: Screenshot of an example HHH (Helpful, Harmless, Honest) system prompt used by Anthropic to guide model behavior, showing the structured natural-language instructions that define desired assistant properties.
teaching_value: Provides a concrete, real-world example of prompt-based behavioral control, helping students understand how alignment objectives can be operationalized as text instructions rather than only through RLHF.
when_to_show: Show when introducing guardrail strategies, specifically to illustrate prompt-based control before discussing output validation approaches.
suggested_caption: Anthropic's HHH prompt guides models toward helpful, harmless, honest responses. (Source: Anthropic)

alt: An example of an input guardrail on Midjourney
description: Screenshot of a Midjourney error message triggered when a user attempts to generate NSFW content, demonstrating an input guardrail blocking an inappropriate prompt before the model processes it.
teaching_value: Gives students a tangible, product-level example of input guardrails in action, making the abstract concept of pre-generation filtering concrete and showing the user-facing consequence of a blocked request.
when_to_show: Show when explaining input guardrail types; use as a real-world case study to ground the distinction between input vs. output guardrails.
suggested_caption: Midjourney blocks NSFW prompts with an input guardrail error message. (Source: Midjourney)

alt: Guidelines for Human-AI interaction across the user journey
description: A 2x2 or timeline-style framework diagram organizing Microsoft's 18 Human-AI Interaction Guidelines across phases of the user journey (initially, during interaction, when wrong, over time), with each guideline shown as a short action rule.
teaching_value: Helps students see that responsible AI UX is structured and phase-dependent — guidelines are not generic but tied to specific moments in the user journey, enabling systematic defensive UX design.
when_to_show: Show when teaching defensive UX or responsible AI product design; use as a framework students can apply when evaluating or designing LLM-powered interfaces.
suggested_caption: Microsoft's 18 Human-AI guidelines organized across the user journey. (Source: Microsoft)

alt: Example of a disclaimer on Google Bard results (Note: The code provided will not work.)
description: Screenshot of Google Bard's UI showing a disclaimer banner warning users that Bard may display inaccurate or offensive information, with a note that the code provided will not work (nrows is not a valid argument).
teaching_value: Provides a concrete product example of expectation-setting through UI disclaimers, illustrating how transparency about model limitations is implemented in practice to build long-term user trust.
when_to_show: Show when discussing how to communicate model limitations to users; use to contrast short-term trust reduction with long-term trust building through transparency.
suggested_caption: Google Bard displays a disclaimer to set user expectations about AI accuracy. (Source: Google Bard)

alt: >Example of collecting user feedback as part of the UX
description: Screenshot of Midjourney's image generation UI showing the feedback buttons: U (upscale/download), V (variation), and regenerate icons beneath generated images, illustrating how explicit user feedback is embedded in the product workflow.
teaching_value: Shows students how implicit and explicit feedback signals (upscale = strong positive, variation = mild positive, regenerate = negative) can be designed into UX to collect preference data for model improvement without separate surveys.
when_to_show: Show when teaching how to collect human preference data for fine-tuning; use to illustrate how product UX can double as a data collection mechanism.
suggested_caption: Midjourney embeds feedback signals (upscale, variation, regenerate) into its UX. (Source: Midjourney)

alt: LLM patterns across the axis of data to user, and defensive to offensive.
description: A 2x2 matrix diagram plotting LLM design patterns on two axes: data-to-user (horizontal) and defensive-to-offensive (vertical), positioning patterns like RAG, caching, fine-tuning, guardrails, and defensive UX in their respective quadrants.
teaching_value: Provides a unifying mental model for the entire article — students can see how all discussed patterns relate to each other along two key dimensions, enabling them to reason about which patterns to apply in different product contexts.
when_to_show: Show at the start of a lesson as a roadmap, or at the end as a synthesis activity; use to help students categorize and compare patterns they have learned.
suggested_caption: LLM patterns mapped across data-to-user and defensive-to-offensive axes. (Source: Eugene Yan)

description: A timeline-style diagram illustrating LangGraph persistence via checkpoints: a graph run progresses through steps/nodes, and at each step a checkpoint is written to a persistent store so execution can be resumed later.
teaching_value: Helps learners visualize what a 'checkpoint' is in LangGraph—discrete saved snapshots of graph state over time—so they understand how persistence enables resume, debugging, and reproducibility.
when_to_show: Show when introducing persistence/checkpointers or when a learner asks how LangGraph can resume after interruption.
suggested_caption: Checkpoints persist graph state over time (LangChain docs).

description: A diagram showing retrieval of state history from a persistent store: multiple past checkpoints are listed/queried to inspect prior states of a LangGraph run.
teaching_value: Clarifies that persistence is not just 'latest state'—students can query historical checkpoints to audit execution, compare intermediate states, and debug where behavior changed.
when_to_show: Show when teaching how to inspect prior steps, debug unexpected outputs, or explain how to list/get checkpoint history.
suggested_caption: Retrieve and inspect prior checkpoint states (LangChain docs).

description: A replay diagram depicting re-running a LangGraph execution from a selected checkpoint, continuing forward from that saved state rather than starting from scratch.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the practical benefit of checkpoints: learners see how replay enables deterministic re-execution from an intermediate point for debugging, evaluation, or branching experiments.
when_to_show: Show when a learner asks how to reproduce a run, continue after failure, or rerun from a mid-graph step.
suggested_caption: Replay execution starting from a saved checkpoint (LangChain docs).

description: A fuller story diagram showing updating state: an existing checkpointed state is modified (patched) and then subsequent execution continues using the updated state, producing a new sequence of checkpoints.
teaching_value: Teaches that persisted state can be programmatically edited to correct inputs, inject new information, or test alternatives—then continue execution—supporting iterative development and what-if analysis.
when_to_show: Show when teaching how to correct or override state mid-run, create a forked run, or demonstrate 'update_state' workflows.
suggested_caption: Update a checkpointed state and continue execution (LangChain docs).

description: A 2D design-space diagram mapping human oversight strategies for computer-use agents along two axes: delegation structure (who has default decision authority: agent-led vs human-controlled) and engagement level (where oversight is organized: high-level plans vs low-level action traces). The four strategy regions correspond to Risk-Gated, Supervisory Co-Execution, Action Confirmation, and Structurally Enriched approaches.
teaching_value: Helps learners compare oversight approaches as structural coordination choices, clarifying tradeoffs between autonomy vs control and plan-level vs action-level monitoring.
when_to_show: Show when introducing oversight strategy taxonomy or when a student is confusing interface features with underlying coordination structures.
suggested_caption: Oversight strategy design space by delegation and engagement level (arXiv).

description: A multi-panel figure showing UI examples for four oversight strategies: (1) Risk-Gated with agent focus/reasoning and a risk-triggered approval dialog; (2) Supervisory Co-Execution with plan review, hierarchical execution trace, and next-step approval; (3) Action Confirmation with per-action confirmation dialogs; (4) Structurally Enriched combining plan review, focus/reasoning with risk-gated approvals, step-level trace with inspectable risk labels, and a plan studio for progress review and plan revision.
teaching_value: Connects abstract oversight strategies to concrete interface mechanisms, helping students see how different approval and monitoring points change user workload and error exposure.
when_to_show: Show after presenting the design-space axes, or when discussing how to implement oversight in UI for agentic web automation.
suggested_caption: Interface instantiations of four CUA oversight strategies (arXiv).

description: A grid of stacked bar charts showing distributions of Likert responses for subjective measures (Workload on 7-point; Control, Trust, Usability on 5-point scales) split by task context (Lower vs Higher context) and oversight strategy (Action Confirmation, Risk Gated, Supervisory Co-Execution, Structurally Enriched). Each bar segment represents the percentage in each response category; negative-worded items were reverse-scored before aggregation.
teaching_value: Teaches how to read and compare response distributions across experimental conditions, emphasizing that effects can appear as shifts in distribution shape rather than just mean differences.
when_to_show: Show when a student needs practice interpreting HCI study results or comparing subjective metrics across multiple conditions and context levels.
suggested_caption: Subjective rating distributions by context and oversight strategy (arXiv).
